title: Authors and Citation

code: @Manual{,   title = {multipleOutcomes: Joint Covariance and Treatment-Effect Tests for Multiple Outcomes},   author = {Han Zhang},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.16},   url = {https://github.com/zhangh12/multipleOutcomes}, }

what: Joint Covariance and Treatment-Effect Tests for Multiple Outcomes
title: Joint Covariance and Treatment-Effect Tests for Multiple Outcomes
text: goal multipleOutcomes fit statistical models multiple outcomes simultaneously. computes estimates parameters across fitted models returns matrix asymptotic covariance. Various applications package, including PATED (Prognostic Variables Assisted Treatment Effect Detection), multiple comparison adjustment, conditional power illustrated.

title: Joint Covariance and Treatment-Effect Tests for Multiple Outcomes
text: Install development version GitHub:
code: if(!requireNamespace("remotes", quietly = TRUE)) {   install.packages("remotes") } remotes::install_github(   "zhangh12/multipleOutcomes",   dependencies = TRUE )

title: Joint Covariance and Treatment-Effect Tests for Multiple Outcomes
text: Let’s get started analyzing real randomized trial data. data randomized clinical trial indomethacin prevent post-ERCP pancreatitis. binary endpoint post-ERCP pancreatitis (yes/). collected, reformatted shared Dr. Peter Higgins R package medicaldata. information, please refer data set indo_rct medicaldata, manuscript. use PATED method estimate marginal treatment effect post-ERCP pancreatitis, adjusting set risk factors PEP potential prognostic covariates. expert disease, simply follow description information covariates medicaldata. compare PATED unconditional logistic regression model estimating treatment effect. covariates, regression model covar ~ rx fitted compute slope coefficient b̂\widehat b. correlation b̂\widehat b β̂\widehat\beta, unadjusted estimate treatment effect (outcome ~ rx), computed column corr.  plot shows covariates well balanced two arms (.e. z-values grey points close zero). evident PATED produces treatment effect estimate (-0.7514) close one unconditional logistic regression model (-0.7051), indicating good property unbiasness. Meanwhile, slightly lower SE (0.247 v.s. 0.2528) significant p-value (0.0023 v.s. 0.0053) obtained PATED. comparison, adjust list covariates logistic regression estimate conditional log odds ratio treatment. exclude asa81 asa due missing data, prophystent, therastent pdstent due numerical instability. conditional treatment effect estimated -0.8011, SE 0.2634 p-value 0.0024. example, seems like using unconditional logistic regression PATED can underestimate treatment effect compared regression adjustment. However, always case practice. also see examples regression adjustment can underestimate treatment effect substantially. fact, comparison invalid estimating different parameters different definitions treatment effect. terms statistical power detecting treatment effect, comprehensive simulation studies show PATED always outperform unconditional logistic regression, comparable (better ) regression adjustment.
code: library(multipleOutcomes) library(dplyr)  options(digits = 4)  data(indo)  # jointCovariance() and pated() need a per-row subject identifier in # column `pid`. The trial data already has an integer `id` — we just # stamp it as a string under the conventional name. indo <- indo %>% mutate(pid = paste0("s-", id))  # When there's only one dataset, `data_index` defaults to 1 and the # `list()` wrap is auto-applied, so neither needs to be spelled out. fit <- pated(   glm_(outcome     ~ rx, family = "binomial"),    glm_(risk        ~ rx, family = "gaussian"),   glm_(gender      ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(sod         ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(pep         ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(recpanc     ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(psphinc     ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(precut      ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(difcan      ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(amp         ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(paninj      ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(acinar      ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(asa81       ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(asa         ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(prophystent ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(therastent  ~ rx, family = "binomial"),   glm_(pdstent     ~ rx, family = "binomial"),    data = indo )  plot(fit) print(fit) #>           term family  estimate  stderr   pvalue     method      corr #> 1      outcome  PATED -0.751357 0.24697 0.002348      PATED        NA #> 2      outcome    glm -0.705130 0.25283 0.005287   Standard  1.000000 #> 3         risk    glm  0.083338 0.07168 0.244984 Prognostic  0.139678 #> 4          pep    glm -0.002142 0.22269 0.992326 Prognostic  0.126343 #> 5          amp    glm  0.041102 0.47862 0.931565 Prognostic  0.082925 #> 6       acinar    glm  0.275329 0.39618 0.487084 Prognostic  0.057366 #> 7       paninj    glm -0.360946 0.27417 0.188011 Prognostic  0.053484 #> 8   therastent    glm -0.252931 0.31591 0.423343 Prognostic -0.049425 #> 9       difcan    glm  0.105662 0.18570 0.569368 Prognostic  0.048068 #> 10     psphinc    glm  0.133453 0.16480 0.418059 Prognostic  0.037479 #> 11 prophystent    glm  0.215272 0.18961 0.256226 Prognostic  0.034128 #> 12     recpanc    glm -0.069990 0.17817 0.694454 Prognostic  0.033145 #> 13         asa    glm -0.072330 0.27877 0.795278 Prognostic -0.024236 #> 14         sod    glm  0.248237 0.21453 0.247228 Prognostic -0.010506 #> 15       asa81    glm -0.394693 0.31587 0.211467 Prognostic -0.007874 #> 16     pdstent    glm  0.181177 0.21480 0.398967 Prognostic  0.006305 #> 17      precut    glm -0.090072 0.36402 0.804570 Prognostic  0.005952 #> 18      gender    glm  0.170977 0.20058 0.393994 Prognostic  0.004055 logit <- glm(   outcome ~ rx + risk + gender + sod + pep + recpanc + psphinc + precut + difcan + amp + paninj + acinar,    family = 'binomial', data = indo ) |> summary()  logit #>  #> Call: #> glm(formula = outcome ~ rx + risk + gender + sod + pep + recpanc +  #>     psphinc + precut + difcan + amp + paninj + acinar, family = "binomial",  #>     data = indo) #>  #> Coefficients: #>              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)     #> (Intercept)  -2.78401    0.47052   -5.92  3.3e-09 *** #> rx           -0.80110    0.26337   -3.04   0.0024 **  #> risk          0.15917    0.54108    0.29   0.7686     #> gender2_male -0.00483    0.35756   -0.01   0.9892     #> sod1_yes      0.33084    0.72780    0.45   0.6494     #> pep1_yes      0.98179    0.63710    1.54   0.1233     #> recpanc1_yes  0.01492    0.38860    0.04   0.9694     #> psphinc1_yes  0.11582    0.59986    0.19   0.8469     #> precut1_yes   0.20988    0.75567    0.28   0.7812     #> difcan1_yes   0.41110    0.61653    0.67   0.5049     #> amp1_yes      1.92303    0.85627    2.25   0.0247 *   #> paninj1_yes   0.16629    0.47729    0.35   0.7275     #> acinar1_yes   0.98954    0.61083    1.62   0.1052     #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1) #>  #>     Null deviance: 468.01  on 601  degrees of freedom #> Residual deviance: 433.43  on 589  degrees of freedom #> AIC: 459.4 #>  #> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5

title: ACTG 320 Clinical Trial Dataset — actg
text: actg dataset Hosmer et al.

title: ACTG 320 Clinical Trial Dataset — actg
text: data frame id Identification Code time Time AIDS diagnosis death (days). censor Event indicator. 1 = AIDS defining diagnosis, 0 = Otherwise. time_d Time death (days) censor_d Event indicator death (). 1 = Death, 0 = Otherwise. tx Treatment indicator. 1 = Treatment includes IDV, 0 = Control group. txgrp Treatment group indicator. 1 = ZDV + 3TC. 2 = ZDV + 3TC + IDV. 3 = d4T + 3TC. 4 = d4T + 3TC + IDV. strat2 CD4 stratum screening. 0 = CD4 <= 50. 1 = CD4 > 50. sex 0 = Male. 1 = Female. raceth Race/Ethnicity. 1 = White Non-Hispanic. 2 = Black Non-Hispanic. 3 = Hispanic. 4 = Asian, Pacific Islander. 5 = American Indian, Alaskan Native. 6 = /unknown. ivdrug IV drug use history. 1 = Never. 2 = Currently. 3 = Previously. hemophil Hemophiliac. 1 = Yes. 0 = . karnof Karnofsky Performance Scale. 100 = Normal; complaint evidence disease. 90 = Normal activity possible; minor signs/symptoms disease. 80 = Normal activity effort; signs/symptoms disease. 70 = Cares self; normal activity/active work possible. cd4 Baseline CD4 count (Cells/Milliliter). priorzdv Months prior ZDV use (months). age Age Enrollment (years).

what: Fitting Regression Models for Multiple Outcomes and Returning the Matrix of Covariance — jointCovariance
title: Fitting Regression Models for Multiple Outcomes and Returning the Matrix of Covariance — jointCovariance
text: jointCovariance can fit different types models multiple outcomes simultaneously return model parameters variance-covariance matrix analysis.

title: Fitting Regression Models for Multiple Outcomes and Returning the Matrix of Covariance — jointCovariance
code: jointCovariance(..., data, nboot = 0, compute_cov = TRUE, seed = NULL)

title: Fitting Regression Models for Multiple Outcomes and Returning the Matrix of Covariance — jointCovariance
text: ... objects returned glm_(), coxph_(), logrank_(), gee_() gmm_(). data data frame models fitted dataset; otherwise list data frames fitting models .... Note dataset can used fit multiple models, thus, length(data) unnecessary equal number models .... row names data frame treated subject IDs. Instead, data frame consist column pid subject IDs. two records different data frames correspond subject, values pid consistent. data frames used GEE, pid defines clusters. See id Details section gee:gee. nboot non-zero integer bootstrap adopted. default 0. compute_cov logic. TRUE nboot > 0, empirical covariance matrix computed using bootstrap estimate returned. Bootstrap estimate abandoned. FALSE, bootstrap estimate returned empirical covariance matrix computed. seed random seed generate bootstrap data.

title: Fitting Regression Models for Multiple Outcomes and Returning the Matrix of Covariance — jointCovariance
text: returns object class "jointCovariance", list containing following components:

title: Fitting Regression Models for Multiple Outcomes and Returning the Matrix of Covariance — jointCovariance
code: ## More examples can be found in the vignettes. library(survival) library(mvtnorm) library(tidyr) genData <- function(seed = NULL){      set.seed(seed)   n <- 300   sigma <- matrix(.7, 4, 4)   diag(sigma) <- 1   v <- rmvnorm(n, sigma = sigma)   x1 <- v[, 1]   x2 <- v[, 2]   z1 <- (v[, 3] > 0) + 0   z2 <- v[, 4]      trt <- rbinom(n, 1, .5)      bet <- c(-.3,.3)   y <- -log(runif(n))/     exp(-.3 * x1 + .3 * x2 + z1 * .5 - z2 * .3 + .1 * trt + rnorm(n))      z1[sample.int(n, 50)] <- NA   z2[sample.int(n, 50)] <- NA   x1[sample.int(n, 50)] <- NA   x2[sample.int(n, 50)] <- NA   death <- ifelse(y > 2, 0, 1)   y[y > 2] <- 2      pid <- paste0('pid-', 1:n)   ret <- data.frame(     y = y, trt = trt,      z1 = z1, z2 = z2,      x1 = x1, x2 = x2,      death, pid)   ret }  dat1 <- genData()  ## create a dataset with repeated measurements x dat2 <- dat1 %>% pivot_longer(c(x1, x2), names_to='visit', values_to='x') %>%    dplyr::select(x, trt, visit, pid) %>% as.data.frame()  dat2$visit <- as.factor(dat2$visit) dat2$pid <- as.factor(dat2$pid)  fit <- jointCovariance(   coxph_(Surv(time = y, event = death) ~ trt, data_index = 1),   logrank_(Surv(time = y, event=death) ~ trt, data_index = 1),   glm_(z1 ~ trt, family = 'binomial', data_index = 1),    glm_(z2 ~ trt, family = 'gaussian', data_index = 1),    mmrm_(x ~ trt + us(visit | pid), reml = TRUE, data_index = 2),    gee_(x ~ trt, family = 'gaussian', corstr = 'independence', data_index = 2),    data = list(dat1, dat2)) #> mmrm() registered as car::Anova extension #> method in mmrm_control is re-set to be "Residual" #> vcov in mmrm_control is re-set to be "Empirical"  fit #> $coefficients #>  [1]  0.21348514  1.70094701  0.08855340  0.22045144 -0.01972026  0.15608819 #>  [7] -0.04804925  0.01592017 -0.06886867  0.02893518 #>  #> $mcov #>                [,1]         [,2]         [,3]         [,4]          [,5] #>  [1,]  0.0156802690  0.124006569 -0.002199680  0.004035362  0.0004939039 #>  [2,]  0.1240065686  0.982375107 -0.017113039  0.031871156  0.0036013938 #>  [3,] -0.0021996805 -0.017113039  0.035467671 -0.035467671  0.0077457149 #>  [4,]  0.0040353618  0.031871156 -0.035467671  0.065367278 -0.0077447718 #>  [5,]  0.0004939039  0.003601394  0.007745715 -0.007744772  0.0084994244 #>  [6,] -0.0010220752 -0.007921925 -0.007750579  0.015961143 -0.0084994244 #>  [7,]  0.0002576265  0.001927432  0.007416328 -0.007416054  0.0049762917 #>  [8,] -0.0007161122 -0.005752429 -0.007411753  0.015305818 -0.0049739764 #>  [9,]  0.0002927342  0.002235022  0.007653351 -0.007653047  0.0052518070 #> [10,] -0.0006166250 -0.004970783 -0.007648251  0.015592438 -0.0052493446 #>               [,6]          [,7]          [,8]          [,9]        [,10] #>  [1,] -0.001022075  0.0002576265 -0.0007161122  0.0002927342 -0.000616625 #>  [2,] -0.007921925  0.0019274315 -0.0057524290  0.0022350225 -0.004970783 #>  [3,] -0.007750579  0.0074163279 -0.0074117532  0.0076533506 -0.007648251 #>  [4,]  0.015961143 -0.0074160535  0.0153058182 -0.0076530472  0.015592438 #>  [5,] -0.008499424  0.0049762917 -0.0049739764  0.0052518070 -0.005249345 #>  [6,]  0.016766859 -0.0049758104  0.0099420190 -0.0052515066  0.010379892 #>  [7,] -0.004975810  0.0060808183 -0.0060808183  0.0064284522 -0.006428452 #>  [8,]  0.009942019 -0.0060808183  0.0130431718 -0.0064284522  0.013535833 #>  [9,] -0.005251507  0.0064284522 -0.0064284522  0.0070329993 -0.007032999 #> [10,]  0.010379892 -0.0064284522  0.0135358331 -0.0070329993  0.014529294 #>  #> $id_map #> $id_map[[1]] #> trt  #>   1  #>  #> $id_map[[2]] #> trt  #>   2  #>  #> $id_map[[3]] #> (Intercept)         trt  #>           3           4  #>  #> $id_map[[4]] #> (Intercept)         trt  #>           5           6  #>  #> $id_map[[5]] #> (Intercept)         trt  #>           7           8  #>  #> $id_map[[6]] #> (Intercept)         trt  #>           9          10  #>  #>  #> $n_shared_sample_sizes #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] [,6] #> [1,]  300  300  250  250  292  292 #> [2,]  300  300  250  250  292  292 #> [3,]  250  250  250  207  244  244 #> [4,]  250  250  207  250  244  244 #> [5,]  292  292  244  244  292  292 #> [6,]  292  292  244  244  292  292 #>  #> $call #> jointCovariance.default(coxph_(Surv(time = y, event = death) ~  #>     trt, data_index = 1), logrank_(Surv(time = y, event = death) ~  #>     trt, data_index = 1), glm_(z1 ~ trt, family = "binomial",  #>     data_index = 1), glm_(z2 ~ trt, family = "gaussian", data_index = 1),  #>     mmrm_(x ~ trt + us(visit | pid), reml = TRUE, data_index = 2),  #>     gee_(x ~ trt, family = "gaussian", corstr = "independence",  #>         data_index = 2), data = list(dat1, dat2)) #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "jointCovariance"   bfit <-jointCovariance(   coxph_(Surv(time=y, event=death) ~ trt, data_index = 1),   logrank_(Surv(time=y, event=death) ~ trt, data_index = 1),   glm_(z1 ~ trt, family = 'binomial', data_index = 1),   glm_(z2 ~ trt, family = 'gaussian', data_index = 1),   mmrm_(x ~ trt + us(visit | pid), reml = TRUE, data_index = 2),   gee_(x ~ trt, family = 'gaussian', corstr = 'independence', data_index = 2),   data = list(dat1, dat2), nboot = 10) #> method in mmrm_control is re-set to be "Residual" #> vcov in mmrm_control is re-set to be "Empirical"  summary(bfit) #> Call: #> jointCovariance.default(coxph_(Surv(time = y, event = death) ~  #>     trt, data_index = 1), logrank_(Surv(time = y, event = death) ~  #>     trt, data_index = 1), glm_(z1 ~ trt, family = "binomial",  #>     data_index = 1), glm_(z2 ~ trt, family = "gaussian", data_index = 1),  #>     mmrm_(x ~ trt + us(visit | pid), reml = TRUE, data_index = 2),  #>     gee_(x ~ trt, family = "gaussian", corstr = "independence",  #>         data_index = 2), data = list(dat1, dat2), nboot = 10) #>  #> Coefficients: #>                      Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   #> model_1_trt          0.213485   0.118814  1.7968  0.07237 . #> model_2_trt          1.700947   0.940847  1.8079  0.07062 . #> model_3_(Intercept)  0.088553   0.161753  0.5475  0.58406   #> model_3_trt          0.220451   0.202356  1.0894  0.27597   #> model_4_(Intercept) -0.019720   0.090206 -0.2186  0.82695   #> model_4_trt          0.156088   0.112528  1.3871  0.16541   #> model_5_(Intercept) -0.048049   0.102719 -0.4678  0.63995   #> model_5_trt          0.015920   0.132841  0.1198  0.90461   #> model_6_(Intercept) -0.068869   0.106670 -0.6456  0.51852   #> model_6_trt          0.028935   0.130144  0.2223  0.82406   #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1  ## km_() and quantile_() require nboot > 0 because they have no ## closed-form score. compute_cov is forced to FALSE for km_(). ## When all models share one dataset, `data_index` and `list(...)` ## can be omitted. kfit <- jointCovariance(   km_(Surv(time = y, event = death) ~ trt, conf_type = 'log',       times = c(0.5, 1, 1.5)),   glm_(z1 ~ trt, family = 'binomial'),   data = dat1, nboot = 30, seed = 1) #> compute_cov is set to FALSE as km_ is in use. You can compute the covariance matrix using $bootstrap_estimate.   qfit <- jointCovariance(   quantile_(y ~ trt, probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75)),   glm_(z2 ~ trt, family = 'gaussian'),   data = dat1, nboot = 30, seed = 1)

what: Creating Objects of Kaplan-Meier Curve — km_
title: Creating Objects of Kaplan-Meier Curve — km_
text: km_ wrapper function creating object Kaplan-Meier curve passed jointCovariance, main function package argument .... object defines Kaplan-Meier curve fitted.

title: Creating Objects of Kaplan-Meier Curve — km_
code: km_(formula, times = NULL, conf_type, data_index = 1)

title: Creating Objects of Kaplan-Meier Curve — km_
text: formula formula created survival::Surv(). times numeric vector time. Survival probabilities times computed (g-transformation defined conf_type). conf_type character. Type confidence interval. must one "log", "log-log", "plain", "logit", "arcsin". data_index integer. Index data frame data argument jointCovariance used fitting generalized linear model.

title: Creating Objects of Kaplan-Meier Curve — km_
text: Usually, g-transformation applied survival probability S(t) obtain pointwise confidence interval Kaplan-Meier curve. can achieved specifying conf_type. identity transformation, use conf_type = "plain". function can used jointCovariance bootstrap method used estimate variance-covariance matrix multiple outcome models.

what: Creating Objects of Logrank Test — logrank_

code: logrank_(formula, ties = c("efron", "breslow", "exact"), data_index = 1)

title: Creating Objects of Logrank Test — logrank_
text: formula see formula survival::coxph. data_index integer. Index data frame data argument jointCovariance used computing testing statistic logrank test.

title: Creating Objects of Logrank Test — logrank_
text: arguments survival::coxph supported logrank_ due complexity handling environment scope, particularly difficult arguments like weights, subset, etc.

title: Creating Objects of Mixed Models for Repeated Measures — mmrm_
text: argument weights mmrm::mmrm supported mmrm_ due complexity handling environment scope. Please always refer help document mmrm::mmrm using mmrm_. example, time variable observation ID must factor variables cases, otherwise error may prompted. Users can call mmrm::mmrm using arguments passed mmrm_ check validity.

what: Prognostic Variables Assisted Treatment Effect Detection — pated
title: Prognostic Variables Assisted Treatment Effect Detection — pated
text: pated wrapper function jointCovariance testing treatment effect randomized clinical trials. assumes prognostic variables fully randomized. assumption can help enhancing statistical power conventional approaches detecting treatment effect. Specifically, sensitivity conventional models specified ... improved pated.

title: Prognostic Variables Assisted Treatment Effect Detection — pated
code: pated(   ...,   data,   nboot = 0,   compute_cov = TRUE,   seed = NULL,   transform = "identity" )

title: Prognostic Variables Assisted Treatment Effect Detection — pated
text: ... model specifications built glm_(), coxph_(), logrank_(), gee_(), mmrm_(), km_(), quantile_(). first specification primary outcome whose treatment effect tested; rest prognostic covariates used tighten SE. data either single data frame (models fitted dataset) list data frames (one entry per data_index). data frame must pid column carrying subject identifiers; records pid across different data frames refer subject. nboot non-zero integer bootstrap adopted. default 0. compute_cov logic. TRUE, empirical covariance matrix computed using bootstrap estimate returned. Bootstrap estimate abandoned. FALSE, bootstrap estimate returned empirical covariance matrix computed. seed random seed generate bootstrap data. transform character. Now supports "identity".

title: Prognostic Variables Assisted Treatment Effect Detection — pated
text: data frame testing results.

title: Prognostic Variables Assisted Treatment Effect Detection — pated
code: ## More examples can be found in the vignettes. library(survival) library(mvtnorm)  genData <- function(seed = NULL){   set.seed(seed)   n <- 300   sigma <- matrix(c(1, .6, .6, 1), 2)   x <- rmvnorm(n, sigma = sigma)   z1 <- rbinom(n, 1, .6)   z2 <- rnorm(n)   trt <- rbinom(n, 1, .5)    bet <- c(-.2, .2)   y <- -.5 + x %*% bet + z1 * .3 - z2 * .1 + .1 * trt - .1 * rnorm(n)   death <- rbinom(n, 1, .8)   data.frame(     y = as.numeric(y), trt = trt,     z1 = z1, z2 = z2,     x1 = x[, 1], x2 = x[, 2],     death, pid = paste0('s-', seq_len(n))   ) }  dat <- genData(seed = 31415926)  ## `data_index` defaults to 1 in every spec constructor and a single ## data.frame is auto-wrapped into a list, so neither needs spelling out ## when all models are fitted on the same dataset. fit <-   pated(     coxph_(Surv(time = y, event = death) ~ trt),     glm_(z1 ~ trt, family = 'binomial'),     glm_(z2 ~ trt, family = 'gaussian'),     glm_(x1 ~ trt, family = 'gaussian'),     glm_(x2 ~ trt, family = 'gaussian'),     data = dat   )  fit #>                            term family     estimate     stderr       pvalue #> 1 Surv(time = y, event = death)  PATED -0.288684461 0.08056904 0.0003395805 #> 2 Surv(time = y, event = death)  coxph -0.201743178 0.13062378 0.1224769862 #> 3                            z1    glm -0.269021294 0.23351160 0.2492930398 #> 4                            z2    glm  0.110597580 0.11184325 0.3227304829 #> 5                            x2    glm  0.039205611 0.11165593 0.7254917405 #> 6                            x1    glm  0.007879826 0.11228370 0.9440521271 #>       method       corr #> 1      PATED         NA #> 2   Standard  1.0000000 #> 3 Prognostic -0.4589612 #> 4 Prognostic  0.4075322 #> 5 Prognostic -0.3087809 #> 6 Prognostic  0.2148699

title: multipleOutcomes 0.16
text: multipleOutcomes() legacy *MO() wrappers (coxphMO, glmMO, geeMO, logrankMO, kmMO, quantileMO) removed. package’s main entry point now jointCovariance(), component model specified constructor: glm_(), coxph_(), logrank_(), gee_(), mmrm_(), km_(), quantile_(). pated() accepts spec constructors via .... Datasets supplied jointCovariance() / pated() must now contain column pid carrying subject identifiers. Records pid across different data frames refer subject.

title: multipleOutcomes 0.16
text: mmrm_() adapter mixed models repeated measures. km_() adapter Kaplan-Meier survival probabilities (bootstrap-, since empirical S(t) closed-form score). quantile_() adapter -arm quantile differences (bootstrap-). gee_() adapter exposing GEE fit’s per-cluster score Hessian slots joint asymptotic covariance machinery alongside engines. conf_type = "arcsin" fully supported km_() (previously silently fell back logit transformation).

title: multipleOutcomes 0.16
text: data_index defaults 1 every spec constructor, single data frame auto-wrapped, jointCovariance(spec1(...), spec2(...), data = my_df) shortest valid call. Spec constructors validate data_index (must positive integer scalar); jointCovariance() adds upfront bounds check naming offending spec data_index exceeds number supplied data frames. pated() emits warning residual variance goes negative — typically sign prognostic variable collinear primary outcome another prognostic.

title: multipleOutcomes 0.16
text: KMAdapter$fit_model() longer strips names self$estimate, id_map entries KM models now carry time_(strata)_(time) labels pated()’s arm lookup relies . parseTreatmentVariableFromCall() now walks formula AST instead regex-parsing deparsed call, formulas nested parentheses (e.g., y ~ arm + us(visit | pid) mmrm_) parse correctly. fitKMCurve() uses survival::summary(..., extend = TRUE) consistently, preventing NAs leaking bootstrap covariance matrix resample’s stratum observations past requested time. pated() longer relies .null(family), silently bound stats::family (function) family argument removed.

title: multipleOutcomes 0.16
text: New testthat suite (52 tests / 245 expectations) covering every engine jointCovariance() pated(). Tests split fast tier (~4 s) Monte Carlo tier (~25 s, opt-via MULTIPLEOUTCOMES_RUN_MC=1) validates empirical vs. theoretical covariance single-engine, cross-engine, partial-overlap, kitchen-sink configurations. Regression fixture README example pinned 1e-10 (file inst/testdata/readme_pated_reference.rds). GitHub Actions workflows code coverage (codecov) pkgdown. README example, vignette, help-page examples migrated new spec-constructor API.

title: multipleOutcomes 0.15
text: Initial support Kaplan-Meier new spec-constructor interface via km_(). pated() extended handle KM time-stratum parameter vectors, including transformed-S(t) point estimates, pointwise confidence intervals, KM-vs-PATED curve comparison plots.

title: multipleOutcomes 0.14 and earlier
text: See git history.
